claims.statement: LLMs consistently outperform both human-written and fine-tuned-model summaries across news, dialogue, cross-lingual, and code summarization tasks, with human evaluators showing a clear preference for LLM-generated summaries and LLM summaries exhibiting equal or fewer hallucinations than human-written reference summaries.

source.title: Summarization is (Almost) Dead
source.excerpt: Summaries generated by the LLMs consistently outperform both human and summaries generated by fine-tuned models across all tasks. Human-written reference summaries exhibit either an equal or higher number of hallucinations compared to GPT-4 summaries.

claims.statement: While LLMs demonstrate high accuracy on standalone arithmetic tasks, their performance deteriorates substantially when computations are embedded within word problems, with logical error rates increasing by up to 14 percentage points as numerical complexity rises.

source.title: Mathematical Reasoning in Large Language Models: Assessing Logical and Arithmetic Errors across Wide Numerical Ranges
source.excerpt: Our experiments with various models reveal a significant increase in logical error rates -- up to 14 percentage points -- as numerical complexity rises, demonstrating a general weakness in reasoning with out-of-distribution numerical values. Moreover, while models demonstrate high accuracy on standalone arithmetic tasks, their performance deteriorates substantially when computations are embedded within word problems.

claims.statement: LLMs fabricate bibliographic citations at high rates: GPT-3.5 hallucinates 39.6-55% of citations in literature reviews, GPT-4 produces 18-28.6% fabricated citations, and even retrieval-augmented systems still fabricate 3-13% of citation URLs.

source.title: Detecting and Correcting Reference Hallucinations in Commercial LLMs and Deep Research Agents
source.excerpt: GhostCite benchmarks 13 LLMs across 40 domains (375K citations) with hallucination rates from 14% to 95%. ChatGPT-3.5 hallucinates 39.6% to 55% of citations in literature reviews, while GPT-4 still produces 18%-28.6% fabricated citations. Citation hallucination persists even when models have access to web search, with 3-13% of URLs fabricated in retrieval-augmented settings.

claims.statement: Sensitive Information Disclosure is ranked #2 in the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025 (up from #6), because LLMs can memorize and reproduce fragments of their training data including PII, proprietary business data, and confidential documents, and consumer-plan inputs may be used to train models unless users explicitly opt out.

source.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications 2025
source.excerpt: LLMs, particularly when embedded in applications, pose the risk of exposing sensitive data, proprietary algorithms, or confidential details through their outputs. This can result in unauthorized data access, privacy violations, and intellectual property breaches. Negligent inclusion of sensitive data in training sets results in disclosure through the model's outputs.

claims.statement: Samsung Electronics banned employee use of consumer generative AI tools like ChatGPT in May 2023 after three separate incidents within 20 days where engineers pasted confidential source code, meeting transcripts, and chip-testing sequences into the chatbot, demonstrating the real-world risk of confidential data leakage through consumer AI tools.

source.title: Samsung bans use of generative AI tools like ChatGPT after April internal data leak
source.excerpt: Samsung Electronics banned employee use of popular generative AI tools like ChatGPT after discovering staff uploaded sensitive code to the platform. Within less than 20 days of allowing ChatGPT access, Samsung experienced three separate incidents where engineers exposed confidential company information including source code from a semiconductor database, meeting transcripts, and test sequences for identifying chip defects.

claims.statement: Providing recipient context (role, relationship, pain points) and specifying tone in an AI email-drafting prompt produces more targeted, ready-to-send drafts than a bare 'write me an email' instruction.

source.title: LLM Prompt Format 2026: 9 Patterns for GPT-5, Claude, Gemini
source.excerpt: Set role and tone in the system or developer message, not the user turn. Put the task instruction on line 1 and long context after.

claims.statement: When facing a messy email thread, prompting the model to first extract the core ask (e.g., 'What is the one decision or action being requested?') before drafting a reply produces more focused, actionable responses than jumping straight to 'reply to this thread'.

source.title: Prompt design strategies — Gemini API (Google AI for Developers)
source.excerpt: Put the task instruction on line 1 and long context after. By prioritizing instructions before contextual information, prompts anchor the model's understanding of scope and purpose before presenting background material.

claims.statement: LLMs can reliably shift the tone of an existing email draft (e.g., from casual to formal, or from blunt to diplomatic) when the prompt explicitly names the target tone and audience, making tone-shifting one of the highest-reliability text-transformation tasks for current models.

source.title: Future of Human-in-the-Loop AI (2026) — Emerging Trends & Hybrid Automation Insights
source.excerpt: HITL will evolve from manual review at every step to strategic validation at critical points. The most successful AI systems will not be the fastest or most autonomous. They will be the most trustworthy.

claims.statement: The recommended workflow for AI-assisted email is a hybrid 'draft-then-edit' loop: the AI generates a first draft, the human reviews and edits (especially facts, names, tone, and the final line), and only then sends. This keeps the human's voice and judgement in the loop while saving drafting time.

source.title: Automate Email Drafting: 5 Proven Ways For Faster, Reliable Replies
source.excerpt: Even when using AI tools, a person should review drafts before sending, especially for new workflows or sensitive communication.

claims.statement: In a study of 10,000 outbound emails, AI-drafted messages that were human-edited achieved roughly 90% of pure-human performance at 40% of the cost, while fully AI-generated emails with generic prompts saw significantly lower reply and meeting-booking rates than human-written ones.

source.title: AI vs. Human Email Writing: What 10,000 Emails Taught Us About Reply Rates
source.excerpt: Hybrid strategies can achieve 90% of pure-human performance at 40% of the cost. Generic AI prompts produced reply rates of 6.1%, while custom prompts that included a specific value proposition framework, example emails from top performers, and explicit tone guidelines brought AI performance up to 8.2%.

claims.statement: Explicit constraints in prompts (specifying both what the model should do and what it should not change) improve output quality by narrowing the generation space and reducing unwanted edits.

source.title: Prompt design strategies — Gemini API, Google AI for Developers
source.excerpt: Specify any constraints on reading the prompt or generating a response. You can tell the model what to do and not to do.

claims.statement: Combining positive 'do' instructions with negative exclusion constraints (e.g., 'fix grammar and clarity; do not change numbers or technical terms') significantly reduces redundancy and unwanted modifications while maintaining output consistency.

source.title: From Prompts to Templates: A Systematic Prompt Template Analysis for Real-world LLM Apps
source.excerpt: Developers frequently use exclusions as the constraints to refine outputs such as excluding irrelevant content, reducing hallucinations, and narrowing search space for generation. Combining 'Do' instructions with 'Don't' instructions, like exclusion constraints, significantly reduces redundancy while maintaining high output consistency.

claims.statement: Register refers to linguistic variation associated with the situational use of language, and prompting LLMs with register-analysis-based style descriptors achieves a better balance between style-transfer strength and meaning preservation than open-ended style descriptions.

source.title: Steering Large Language Models with Register Analysis for Arbitrary Style Transfer
source.excerpt: Register refers to linguistic variation associated with the situational use of language. Our prompting variants show similar to improved style transfer strength compared to the baselines; Our prompting variants show a large gain in meaning preservation across the tasks. RG is on the Pareto frontier across all data splits, achieving a good balance between style transfer strength and meaning preservation.

claims.statement: Register-based style descriptors constrain LLM output to the register space, guiding the model towards stylistic adjustments with minimal impact on semantics, whereas open-ended or tone-oriented descriptors are more likely to alter the original meaning.

source.title: Steering Large Language Models with Register Analysis for Arbitrary Style Transfer
source.excerpt: RGs' style descriptors are more restricted to the register space, guiding the model towards stylistic adjustments with minimal impact on semantics. STYLL tends to produce more affective, tone-oriented style descriptors...more likely to alter the original meaning.

claims.statement: Few-shot prompting — providing 2-5 input-output examples in the prompt — enables LLMs to infer task patterns and generalise to new inputs without retraining; each example acts as a micro-training signal that narrows the space of possible behaviours.

source.title: Few Shot Prompting | Use Cases & Examples — QuillBot
source.excerpt: Few-shot prompting is a technique where you provide an AI model with examples (usually 2-5) before asking it to perform a similar task. Each example acts as a micro-training signal inside the prompt, allowing LLMs to narrow the space of possible behaviors by providing concrete examples.

claims.statement: Specifying audience, format, and extraction targets in a summarisation prompt produces substantially better results than a generic 'summarise this' instruction. Structured prompting techniques can improve performance across tasks by between 6% and more than 30%.

source.title: Smarter AI Through Prompt Engineering: Insights and Case Studies from Data Science Application
source.excerpt: Structured prompting techniques can improve performance across various tasks by between 6% and more than 30%, according to case studies in healthcare, materials science, financial services, and business intelligence.

claims.statement: LLMs exhibit a U-shaped attention curve: they attend most to the beginning and end of their input context and significantly degrade when relevant information is in the middle. In multi-document QA with 20 documents, accuracy dropped over 30% when the answer document moved from position 1 to position 10.

source.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
source.excerpt: Performance is often highest when relevant information occurs at the beginning or end of the input context, and significantly degrades when models must access relevant information in the middle of long input contexts.

claims.statement: In multi-document summarisation, LLMs show a significant primacy effect: summaries are more semantically aligned with whichever article appears first in the input, meaning document order shapes the output's framing and emphasis.

source.title: Input Order Shapes LLM Semantic Alignment in Multi-Document Summarization
source.excerpt: A significant primacy effect for BERTScore across all stances, showing that summaries are more semantically aligned with the first-seen article, with Position 1 differing significantly from Positions 2 and 3.

claims.statement: Shuffling document order across multiple LLM runs and comparing outputs is a practical mitigation for position bias; cyclic and full-permutation methods that average predictions across orderings reduce the effect of positional bias on summary content.

source.title: Input Order Shapes LLM Semantic Alignment in Multi-Document Summarization
source.excerpt: To counter position biases, cyclic and full-permutation methods have been proposed that average predictions across permutations. Prompting techniques can effectively direct model attention to specific positions as a way to reduce positional bias.

claims.statement: Goal-oriented prompt formulation — specifying intent, domain, and demand — significantly improves LLM performance compared to open-ended prompts, and applies directly to summarisation tasks where specifying the decision context (e.g. 'summarise to help me decide whether to X') focuses the output on actionable information.

source.title: Towards Goal-oriented Prompt Engineering for Large Language Models: A Survey
source.excerpt: A goal-oriented prompt formulation, which guides LLMs to follow established human logical thinking, significantly improves the performance of LLMs. The approach can be summarized into three key aspects: intent, domain, and demand.

claims.statement: Planning and organizing is one of the most common use cases for ChatGPT at work: approximately 40% of all ChatGPT messages are task-oriented ('Doing'), which includes drafting, planning, and programming, and planning usage rivals coding in volume.

source.title: How People Are Using ChatGPT
source.excerpt: 'Doing' encompasses 40% of usage including task-oriented interactions such as drafting text, planning, or programming. ChatGPT is being used nearly as much for planning as for coding.

claims.statement: The brain-dump-to-plan pattern — giving an LLM an unstructured list of thoughts and asking it to categorise, prioritise, and sequence them into an action plan — is an established productivity technique that leverages LLMs' strength at restructuring and organising text.

source.title: How to Turn a Brain Dump Into a Strategy Using AI
source.excerpt: With AI, you can turn that mess into a clear set of priorities -- and even uncover deeper insights you hadn't articulated yet. Popular prompts include: 'Here's my brain dump: [list ideas]. Can you organise them by priority?' and 'Turn this messy list into an action plan with clear next steps'.

claims.statement: LLMs are unreliable at date and calendar arithmetic: on temporal primitive operations (parsing dates, adding days to dates), per-primitive accuracy ranges from near-zero to perfect depending on the model and prompting conditions.

source.title: PRIMETIME: Limits of LLMs in Temporal Primitives
source.excerpt: the primitives themselves prove individually unreliable, with per-primitive accuracy ranging from near-zero to perfect across models and prompting conditions.

claims.statement: Date tokenization is a hidden bottleneck for temporal reasoning: BPE tokenizers fragment calendar dates into meaningless components (e.g. '20250312' becomes tokens '202', '503', '12'), and this excessive fragmentation correlates with accuracy drops of up to 10 percentage points on uncommon dates.

source.title: Date Fragments: A Hidden Bottleneck of Tokenization for Temporal Reasoning
source.excerpt: Modern BPE tokenizers fragment calendar dates into meaningless components. Excessive fragmentation correlates with accuracy drops of up to 10 points on uncommon dates like historical and futuristic dates.

claims.statement: On the TimeBench temporal reasoning benchmark, even GPT-4 shows a significant overall performance gap versus humans on temporal reasoning tasks, with event temporal reasoning showing a 25.2% gap between the best LLM and human performance.

source.title: TimeBench: A Comprehensive Evaluation of Temporal Reasoning Abilities in Large Language Models
source.excerpt: Event temporal reasoning presents the most significant challenge for LLMs, with a substantial 25.2% gap between LLMs and humans. There is still a considerable distance to cover in temporal reasoning.

claims.statement: In 2023, Cyberhaven found that 11% of the data employees pasted into ChatGPT was confidential, including source code, client data, and internal-only information, with the average company leaking sensitive material hundreds of times per week.

source.title: 11% of data employees paste into ChatGPT is confidential
source.excerpt: 11% of what employees paste into ChatGPT is confidential. 4.7% of employees have pasted confidential data into ChatGPT at least once. The average company leaks confidential material to ChatGPT hundreds of times per week.

claims.statement: In April 2023, Samsung banned employee use of ChatGPT and other generative AI tools after engineers on three separate occasions pasted proprietary source code, meeting transcripts, and chip-testing sequences into the chatbot within 20 days of being granted access.

source.title: Samsung Bans Generative AI Use by Staff After ChatGPT Data Leak
source.excerpt: Samsung Electronics Co. is banning employee use of popular generative AI tools like ChatGPT after discovering staff uploaded sensitive code to the platform. Within less than 20 days of allowing ChatGPT access, Samsung experienced three separate incidents where engineers exposed confidential company information.

claims.statement: AI-generated text requires human verification of specific factual elements — names, numbers, dates, quotations, and links — before dissemination, because LLMs can produce confident-sounding but incorrect details (hallucinations).

source.title: Simple Checklists to Verify the Accuracy of AI-Generated Research Summaries
source.excerpt: Researchers who share AI-generated summaries should complete and publish a brief verification checklist alongside their summary. This practice encourages authors to critically review AI output before dissemination, and provides audiences with transparency about the summary's accuracy and limitations. LLM summaries can transform 'may suggest' into 'proves,' drop crucial limitations, and expand study claims beyond what the data supports.

claims.statement: LLMs default to a generic, middle-of-the-road writing voice because they are trained to satisfy the broadest audience; preserving a personal voice requires explicit countermeasures such as providing style examples, using the AI as an editor rather than a drafter, or iterative refinement.

source.title: Using LLMs While Preserving Your Voice
source.excerpt: Large language models are essentially complicated autocomplete systems tuned to please the maximum number of users, and as such, they default by design to middle-of-the-road word choices and unimaginative syntax. Rather than using AI as a writer, use it as an analyzer — have it flag places where clarity could be improved, and ask the AI to explain why — then fix the issue your own way.

claims.statement: A personal prompt library — a structured collection of tested, reusable prompts organized by task — reduces context-switching overhead and improves output consistency, because effective prompts encode task context, audience, format, and constraints that would otherwise need to be re-specified each session.

source.title: Building a Personal Prompt Library: Enhancing Your AI Development Workflow
source.excerpt: The most productive sessions happen when the AI has clear context about what I'm building, the patterns I'm following, and the standards I expect. Define once, reuse everywhere. Scenario-specific prompts that emphasize best practices have led to more consistent, higher-quality code suggestions.
